var_functions.execute_python:26.raw_text_preview: 'This book, published by Heinemann in its first edition on May 20, 1996, is written in English and is available in paperback format, consisting of 316 pages. It has an ISBN-10 of 0435088688 and an ISB

result.preview: {"sample_text": "This book, published by Heinemann in its first edition on May 20, 1996, is written in English and is", "pattern": "\b(19\\d{2}|20\\d{2})\b", "search_result": null, "findall_result": []}

args.code:
import json
import re

# Debug regex on actual string
sample_text = "This book, published by Heinemann in its first edition on May 20, 1996, is written in English and is"

# Test the pattern
pattern = r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b'
match = re.search(pattern, sample_text)  # Use search instead of findall first
match_full = re.findall(pattern, sample_text)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'sample_text': sample_text[:100],
    'pattern': pattern,
    'search_result': match.group() if match else None,
    'findall_result': match_full,
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Debug regex on actual string
sample_text = "This book, published by Heinemann in its first edition on May 20, 1996, is written in English and is"

# Test the pattern
pattern = r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b'
match = re.search(pattern, sample_text)  # Use search instead of findall first
match_full = re.findall(pattern, sample_text)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'sample_text': sample_text[:100],
    'pattern': pattern,
    'search_result': match.group() if match else None,
    'findall_result': match_full,
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

result.preview: {"books_with_years_count": 191, "total_books": 200, "decade_counts": {"2000s": 47, "1990s": 16, "2010s": 88, "2020s": 21, "1980s": 11, "1940s": 1, "1930s": 4, "1970s": 2, "1920s": 1}, "sample": [{"book_id": "bookid_1", "year": 2004, "decade": "2000s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_2", "year": 1996, "decade": "1990s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_3", "year": 2012, "decade": "2010s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_4", "year": 2013, "decade": "2010s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_5", "year": 2014, "decade": "2010s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_6", "year": 2021, "decade": "2020s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_7", "year": 2004, "decade": "2000s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_8", "year": 2015, "decade": "2010s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_9", "year": 2019, "decade": "2010s"}, {"book_id": "bookid_10", "year": 2004, "decade": "2000s"}]}

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details - FIXED version
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns - proper regex for word boundaries
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            # Only accept years between 1800-2025
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Process all books AND DEBUG
books_with_years = []
for i, book in enumerate(books):
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })
    
    # Debug first few books
    if i < 5:
        print(f"Debug book {book_id}: year={year}, details={details[:80]}")

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample': books_with_years[:10]
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details - FIXED version
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns - proper regex for word boundaries
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            # Only accept years between 1800-2025
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Process all books AND DEBUG
books_with_years = []
for i, book in enumerate(books):
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })
    
    # Debug first few books
    if i < 5:
        print(f"Debug book {book_id}: year={year}, details={details[:80]}")

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample': books_with_years[:10]
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Join reviews with book decades
# Note: review.purchase_id matches books.book_id
print(f"Total reviews: {len(reviews)}")
print(f"Books with year data: {len(book_to_decade)}")

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

no_match_count = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            if purchase_id in book_to_decade:
                decade = book_to_decade[purchase_id]
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                decade_books[decade].add(purchase_id)
            else:
                no_match_count += 1
        except ValueError:
            continue

print(f"Reviews without matching book: {no_match_count}")

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decades': decade_stats[:10],  # Top 10 by avg rating
    'all_decades': sorted([(d, len(decade_books[d]), sum(r)/len(r)) for d, r in decade_ratings.items()], 
                          key=lambda x: x[0])
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Load review data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Join reviews with book decades
# Note: review.purchase_id matches books.book_id
print(f"Total reviews: {len(reviews)}")
print(f"Books with year data: {len(book_to_decade)}")

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

no_match_count = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            if purchase_id in book_to_decade:
                decade = book_to_decade[purchase_id]
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                decade_books[decade].add(purchase_id)
            else:
                no_match_count += 1
        except ValueError:
            continue

print(f"Reviews without matching book: {no_match_count}")

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decades': decade_stats[:10],  # Top 10 by avg rating
    'all_decades': sorted([(d, len(decade_books[d]), sum(r)/len(r)) for d, r in decade_ratings.items()], 
                          key=lambda x: x[0])
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract numeric ids
review_ids = [r.get('purchase_id') for r in reviews if r.get('purchase_id')]
book_ids = [b.get('book_id') for b in books if b.get('book_id')]

# Show samples
print(f"Sample review purchase_ids: {review_ids[:10]}")
print(f"Sample book book_ids: {book_ids[:10]}")
print(f"Sample review id: {review_ids[0]}, numeric part: {review_ids[0].split('_')[1] if review_ids else None}")
print(f"Sample book id: {book_ids[0]}, numeric part: {book_ids[0].split('_')[1] if book_ids else None}")

# Check if any match
review_nums = set(r.split('_')[1] for r in review_ids if '_' in r)
book_nums = set(b.split('_')[1] for b in book_ids if '_' in b)
common = review_nums.intersection(book_nums)

print(f"Number of common numeric IDs: {len(common)}")
print(f"Sample common IDs: {list(common)[:10] if common else 'None'}")

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract numeric ids
review_ids = [r.get('purchase_id') for r in reviews if r.get('purchase_id')]
book_ids = [b.get('book_id') for b in books if b.get('book_id')]

# Show samples
print(f"Sample review purchase_ids: {review_ids[:10]}")
print(f"Sample book book_ids: {book_ids[:10]}")
print(f"Sample review id: {review_ids[0]}, numeric part: {review_ids[0].split('_')[1] if review_ids else None}")
print(f"Sample book id: {book_ids[0]}, numeric part: {book_ids[0].split('_')[1] if book_ids else None}")

# Check if any match
review_nums = set(r.split('_')[1] for r in review_ids if '_' in r)
book_nums = set(b.split('_')[1] for b in book_ids if '_' in b)
common = review_nums.intersection(book_nums)

print(f"Number of common numeric IDs: {len(common)}")
print(f"Sample common IDs: {list(common)[:10] if common else 'None'}")

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

result.preview: {"total_reviews": 1833, "total_books": 200, "sample_review_ids": ["purchaseid_186", "purchaseid_191", "purchaseid_190", "purchaseid_8", "purchaseid_178"], "sample_book_ids": ["bookid_1", "bookid_2", "bookid_3", "bookid_4", "bookid_5"], "common_numeric_ids_count": 200, "sample_common_ids": ["50", "97", "102", "21", "67", "138", "171", "194", "145", "150"]}

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract numeric ids
review_ids = [r.get('purchase_id') for r in reviews if r.get('purchase_id')]
book_ids = [b.get('book_id') for b in books if b.get('book_id')]

# Check if any match
review_nums = set(r.split('_')[1] for r in review_ids if '_' in r)
book_nums = set(b.split('_')[1] for b in book_ids if '_' in b)
common = review_nums.intersection(book_nums)

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'sample_review_ids': review_ids[:5],
    'sample_book_ids': book_ids[:5],
    'common_numeric_ids_count': len(common),
    'sample_common_ids': list(common)[:10] if common else []
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract numeric ids
review_ids = [r.get('purchase_id') for r in reviews if r.get('purchase_id')]
book_ids = [b.get('book_id') for b in books if b.get('book_id')]

# Check if any match
review_nums = set(r.split('_')[1] for r in review_ids if '_' in r)
book_nums = set(b.split('_')[1] for b in book_ids if '_' in b)
common = review_nums.intersection(book_nums)

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'sample_review_ids': review_ids[:5],
    'sample_book_ids': book_ids[:5],
    'common_numeric_ids_count': len(common),
    'sample_common_ids': list(common)[:10] if common else []
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

result.preview: {"matched_reviews": 1808, "books_with_year_data": 191, "decades_with_10plus_books": 5, "top_decade": {"decade": "2020s", "avg_rating": 4.663636363636364, "num_books": 21, "num_reviews": 110}, "all_ranked_decades": [{"decade": "2020s", "avg_rating": 4.663636363636364, "num_books": 21, "num_reviews": 110}, {"decade": "2010s", "avg_rating": 4.608591885441528, "num_books": 88, "num_reviews": 1257}, {"decade": "2000s", "avg_rating": 4.276223776223776, "num_books": 47, "num_reviews": 286}, {"decade": "1980s", "avg_rating": 4.208333333333333, "num_books": 11, "num_reviews": 72}, {"decade": "1990s", "avg_rating": 3.8208955223880596, "num_books": 16, "num_reviews": 67}]}

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping (bookid_N format)
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Map review purchase_id (purchaseid_N) to decade by numeric ID
# Create mapping from numeric ID to decade based on book_to_decade
id_to_decade = {}
for book_id, decade in book_to_decade.items():
    # book_id is like "bookid_1"
    if '_' in book_id:
        num_id = book_id.split('_')[1]
        id_to_decade[num_id] = decade

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

matched_reviews = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str and '_' in purchase_id:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            review_num_id = purchase_id.split('_')[1]
            
            if review_num_id in id_to_decade:
                decade = id_to_decade[review_num_id]
                # Add both the review id and book id to track unique books
                decade_books[decade].add(review_num_id)  # Use numeric ID to track unique books
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                matched_reviews += 1
        except (ValueError, IndexError):
            continue

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'matched_reviews': matched_reviews,
    'books_with_year_data': len(id_to_decade),
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decade': decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None,
    'all_ranked_decades': decade_stats
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping (bookid_N format)
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Map review purchase_id (purchaseid_N) to decade by numeric ID
# Create mapping from numeric ID to decade based on book_to_decade
id_to_decade = {}
for book_id, decade in book_to_decade.items():
    # book_id is like "bookid_1"
    if '_' in book_id:
        num_id = book_id.split('_')[1]
        id_to_decade[num_id] = decade

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

matched_reviews = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str and '_' in purchase_id:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            review_num_id = purchase_id.split('_')[1]
            
            if review_num_id in id_to_decade:
                decade = id_to_decade[review_num_id]
                # Add both the review id and book id to track unique books
                decade_books[decade].add(review_num_id)  # Use numeric ID to track unique books
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                matched_reviews += 1
        except (ValueError, IndexError):
            continue

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'matched_reviews': matched_reviews,
    'books_with_year_data': len(id_to_decade),
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decade': decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None,
    'all_ranked_decades': decade_stats
}))

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:4.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:4.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:4.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
